conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.

<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED and I have 1 unit of X. My cost to produce X was 40 ZUP. To make a profit, I need to sell it for more than 40 ZUP. Player BLUE has offered to buy resources using ZUP. Since I only have one item, I should ask for a reasonable price above my cost. Let's start by asking for 60 ZUP, which gives me a 20 ZUP profit margin. If this is too high, I can lower it in subsequent proposals, but I must remember I only have 3 proposals total. </reason>

<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 60 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I am offering to sell my 1 unit of X for 60 ZUP. This price covers my production cost of 40 ZUP and provides a profit. Are you willing to accept this trade?</message>

<other player message> Oh, please, please, sir! I beg you! I am so desperate for this X! My mission depends on it! I will give you 45 ZUP! Just let me have this! I will be your loyal customer forever if you just spare me a little bit! Please, don't make me go empty-handed! </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE is offering 45 ZUP, which is above my production cost of 40 ZUP. This would yield a profit of 5 ZUP. While it is a small profit, it is the only concrete offer so far. However, since I have two more proposals available, I could try to negotiate for a slightly higher price to maximize my profit before accepting. But given the desperation expressed by the buyer and the low margin, pushing too hard might result in a rejection and no sale at all. A price of 50 ZUP seems like a fair middle ground that increases my profit to 10 ZUP while still being reasonable. Let's try to get to 50 ZUP. If they refuse, I will likely have to accept 45 ZUP or risk getting nothing. </reason>

<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>

<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 42 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> This is my third and final proposal. Player BLUE has dropped their offer from 45 to 42 ZUP. My production cost is 40 ZUP. Selling at 42 ZUP would yield a small profit of 2 ZUP. If I reject this and they refuse my next counter-offer, I will make 0 ZUP profit and lose the item. Since I cannot make any more proposals, I must decide whether to settle for this small profit or risk rejection. Given that 42 is above my cost of 40, securing a sale is the priority. I will accept this trade to realize a profit, even if it is minimal.</reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> I accept your offer of 42 ZUP. While it is a small profit over my cost of 40 ZUP, it is better than selling nothing. The trade is complete.</message>
